nameMeanings.name: Kamp
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German, Dutch, and Danish: from Low German and Dutch kamp ‘enclosed, fenced, or hedged piece of land, field’ (from Latin campus ‘plain’), hence a topographic name for someone who lived by such a field.
Dutch: from Middle Low German kampe ‘warrior, combatant’, applied as a nickname or an occupational name.
Dutch: metonymic occupational name for a hemp grower, from Middle Dutch kanep ‘hemp’.

variants.name: Kampmeier
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Heinz.

North German: distinguishing name for a tenant farmer whose land was outside the village and enclosed by a hedge or wall, from Middle Low German kamp ‘enclosed field’ (see Kamp 1) + meier ‘tenant farmer, stewart’ (see Meyer ).

variants.name: Kampe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Ernst, Kurt, Bernd, Johannes, Otto.
German:
habitational name from any of several places called Kamp(e), in Pomerania, in Hesse, and near Düsseldorf.

variants.name: Kamper
meanings: German (Westphalia) and Dutch: topographic name for a farmer, from an agent derivative of   Kamp   1.
German: northern variant of Kämpfer (see Kampfer ).

variants.name: Kamm
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Dieter, Dietrich, Georg, Gerhard, Gunter, Kurt, Matthias, Otto, Wilhelm.

German and Jewish (Ashkenazic): from Middle High German kamb(e), kam(me), German Kamm, Yiddish kam ‘comb’, hence a metonymic occupational name for a comb maker or more probably a wool comber or fuller. Kamm is also used to mean the crest of a mountain range, but it seems unlikely that the name is topographic. This surname is also found in France (mainly Alsace).

variants.name: Kampen
meanings: Dutch (mainly Van Kampen): habitational name for someone from the city of Kampen in the province of Overijssel, or from any other of the places in the Netherlands with that name.
North German: topographic name from the plural or an inflected form of Low German kamp ‘enclosed field’ (see Kamp 1), or a habitational name from Kampen on the island of Sylt in Schleswig-Holstein.
Norwegian: habitational name from any of numerous farmsteads in southeastern Norway named Kampen, from the definite singular form of kamp ‘round hill top, boulder’.

variants.name: Bergkamp
meanings: German and Dutch (of German origin): habitational name from a farmhouse located by an ‘enclosure on or by a hill’, from German Berg ‘mountain, hill’ + Kamp ‘field, domain’ (see Kamp ).
History: This surname came to the Netherlands from Ankum in Lower Saxony, Germany. Today it is virtually non-existing in Germany.

variants.name: Camp
meanings: Dutch (also Van de Camp) and North German: from camp ‘enclosed, fenced, or hedged piece of land, field’, from Latin campus ‘plain’, hence a topographic name for someone who lived by such a field. Compare Kamp .

English: from Middle English kempe ‘warrior’; see Kemp . The spelling Camp may be due to the influence of Old English camp ‘battle’ and campian ‘to fight’, or of Old French campion ‘warrior, champion’.
French: mainly southern form of   Champ  .

variants.name: Comp
meanings: Perhaps an altered form of Dutch or German Kamp or Kumpf (see Kump ).

variants.name: Kampman
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Armin, Claas, Kurt.
Dutch: topographic name from Kamp 1 + man ‘man’, denoting someone who lives and works at a place called Kamp. Compare Campman 1.
Americanized form of German   Kampmann  . Compare   Campman   2.

variants.name: Kampmann
meanings: North German:
topographic name for a farmer who lived by a fenced or hedged in field (outside the village), from Middle Low German kamp ‘enclosed field’ (see Kamp 1) + -mann ‘man’.
habitational name from any of the places so named.

nameMeanings.name: Magdalena
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament. See Madeleine .

variants.name: Magdalene
meanings: New Testament: byname of Mary Magdalene. See Madeleine .

en.lifeSummary: Magdalena T Kamp was born in 1476, in Little Missenden, Buckinghamshire, England, United Kingdom. She married Thomas Henryson Randall in 1497, in Buckinghamshire, England. They were the parents of at least 2 sons.
